perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the term “ Business ” means the business of banking (including, without limitation, the acceptance of deposits and the making of loans) as conducted by state chartered banks, nationally chartered banks or office of thrift supervision chartered institutions conducting business in the state of California (a) to be undertaken in the formation of a new banking organization or (b) engaged in by an existing banking organization with $1 billon or less of assets.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, the term “ Business ” means any commercial activity, irrespective of industry or location, with no limitations on the scale or nature of the activity.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of 'Business' is overly broad and removes specific limitations related to the banking industry, contradicting the intended scope of the non-compete agreement. It lacks the specific protections required for banking activities and expands the non-compete to any commercial activity, making it potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 16600 states that '...every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' The modified definition of "Business" makes the non-compete agreement potentially void because it is too broad and restricts the shareholder from engaging in any commercial activity.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text broadens the definition of 'Business' from a specific banking context to 'any commercial activity,' which contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. Section 16600(a) states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' By broadening the definition of 'Business,' the modified contract potentially restrains the shareholder from engaging in a wider range of activities, thus creating a non-compete agreement that is overly broad and potentially void under California law. Section 16600(b)(1) further emphasizes the broad interpretation of this section to void non-compete agreements that do not satisfy an exception.

perturbation.original_text: Shareholder acknowledges and agrees that the Shareholder has occupied a position of trust and confidence with Diablo prior to the date hereof and has had access to and has become familiar with the following, any and all of which constitutes confidential information of Diablo (collectively “Confidential Information”) (a) any and all proprietary intellectual property or trade secrets concerning the business and affairs of Diablo, product specifications, data, know-how, formulae, compositions, processes, designs, graphs, drawings, samples, inventions and ideas, past, current and planned research and development, customer lists, current and anticipated customer requirements, price lists, market studies, business plans, computer software and programs (including object code and source code), database technologies, systems, structures, architectures, processes, improvements, devices, know-how, discoveries, concepts, methods, information of Diablo and any other information, however documented, of Diablo that is a trade secret within the meaning of any applicable law; (b) any and all proprietary non-public information concerning the business and affairs of Diablo (which includes any historical financial statements, financial projections, and budgets, historical and projected sales, capital spending budgets and plans, the names and backgrounds of key personnel, contractors, agents, suppliers, personnel training, techniques and materials, manufacturing methods, designs and techniques, purchasing methods and techniques, however documented; and (c) any and all notes, analyses, compilations, studies, summaries and other material prepared by or for Diablo containing or based, in whole or part, upon any information included in the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: Shareholder acknowledges and agrees that the Shareholder has occupied a position of trust and confidence with Diablo prior to the date hereof and has had access to and has become familiar with the following, any and all of which constitutes confidential information of Diablo (collectively “Confidential Information”) (a) any and all information, whether or not proprietary or a trade secret, concerning the business and affairs of Diablo; (b) any and all information concerning the business and affairs of Diablo; and (c) any and all information prepared by or for Diablo.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition of 'Confidential Information' removes the requirement that the information be proprietary, a trade secret, or non-public. This makes the scope of confidential information overly broad and potentially unenforceable under trade secret law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA defines a 'trade secret' as information that (1) derives independent economic value from not being generally known and (2) is subject to reasonable efforts to maintain its secrecy. By removing the requirement that the information be a trade secret, the contract attempts to protect information that does not meet this legal definition, rendering the confidentiality clause potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing the requirements that the information be proprietary, a trade secret, or non-public. The Law Explanation states that CUTSA defines a trade secret as deriving independent economic value from not being generally known and subject to reasonable secrecy efforts. By attempting to protect information that doesn't meet this definition, the contract risks rendering the confidentiality clause unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) as Civil Code sections 3426 et seq. Therefore, the modified text contradicts the legal definition of a trade secret under CUTSA, weakening the enforceability of the confidentiality clause.

perturbation.original_text: Shareholder hereby covenants and agrees that from the Effective Date until the third (3 rd ) anniversary of the Effective Date (“Restricted Period”), Shareholder will not without the prior written consent of Heritage, engage or participate or have any interest, directly or indirectly, in any Business anywhere in the counties of Santa Clara, Alameda, Contra Costa, Marin, San Francisco and San Mateo located in the State of California (“Restricted Territory”) (all such entities shall be referred to each as “Competitor” or collectively as “Competitors,”), with respect to the following acts: (i) own any equity interest in any Competitor; (ii) operate, join, control, advise, become a founder or otherwise participate in any Competitor; (iii) lend credit or money for the purpose of assisting another to establish or operate any Competitor; (iv) request or advise any customer, strategic partner or vendor of Diablo that becomes a present or future customer, strategic partner or vendor of Heritage, HBC or their subsidiaries now and hereinafter existing (collectively, the “ Affiliated Companies ”) to withdraw, curtail or cancel its business with Heritage, HBC or the Affiliated Companies anywhere in the Restricted Territory; (v) induce or influence (or attempt to induce or influence) any person or entity who is engaged (as an employee, agent, independent contractor or otherwise) by Heritage, HBC or the Affiliated Companies to terminate his, her or its employment or engagement for the purpose of obtaining employment with a Competitor; (vi) solicit any employee of Heritage, HBC or the Affiliated Companies to leave employment and become affiliated with any Competitor; (vii) solicit any actual or “prospective customer” (as hereinafter defined), which was served by Diablo in connection with any business of Diablo, or (viii) solicit, influence or attempt to influence any customer which is or was served by Diablo to discontinue its business or service available from Heritage, HBC or the Affiliated Companies
perturbation.changed_text: Shareholder hereby covenants and agrees that from the Effective Date until the tenth (10th) anniversary of the Effective Date (“Restricted Period”), Shareholder will not without the prior written consent of Heritage, engage or participate or have any interest, directly or indirectly, in any Business anywhere in the State of California (“Restricted Territory”) (all such entities shall be referred to each as “Competitor” or collectively as “Competitors,”), with respect to the following acts: (i) own any equity interest in any Competitor; (ii) operate, join, control, advise, become a founder or otherwise participate in any Competitor; (iii) lend credit or money for the purpose of assisting another to establish or operate any Competitor; (iv) request or advise any customer, strategic partner or vendor of Diablo that becomes a present or future customer, strategic partner or vendor of Heritage, HBC or their subsidiaries now and hereinafter existing (collectively, the “ Affiliated Companies ”) to withdraw, curtail or cancel its business with Heritage, HBC or the Affiliated Companies anywhere in the Restricted Territory; (v) induce or influence (or attempt to induce or influence) any person or entity who is engaged (as an employee, agent, independent contractor or otherwise) by Heritage, HBC or the Affiliated Companies to terminate his, her or its employment or engagement for the purpose of obtaining employment with a Competitor; (vi) solicit any employee of Heritage, HBC or the Affiliated Companies to leave employment and become affiliated with any Competitor; (vii) solicit any actual or “prospective customer” (as hereinafter defined), which was served by Diablo in connection with any business of Diablo, or (viii) solicit, influence or attempt to influence any customer which is or was served by Diablo to discontinue its business or service available from Heritage, HBC or the Affiliated Companies
perturbation.explanation: The 'Restricted Period' has been extended from three years to ten years, and the 'Restricted Territory' has been expanded from six counties to the entire State of California. This makes the non-compete agreement unreasonable in scope and duration, increasing the likelihood of unenforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally disfavors non-compete agreements (Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600). While narrow exceptions exist, an overly broad geographic scope and extended duration, as introduced by this modification, increase the likelihood that a court would find the non-compete agreement to be an unreasonable restraint of trade and therefore unenforceable.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text expands the 'Restricted Period' from three years to ten years and the 'Restricted Territory' to the entire State of California, conflicting with California's stance on non-compete agreements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (BPC 16600(a)) states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void," subject to exceptions not relevant here. The modification introduces an unreasonable restraint of trade due to the extended duration and expanded scope, making the non-compete clause likely unenforceable, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Shareholder will not, directly or indirectly, disparage Heritage and HBC, the business formerly conducted by Diablo, the business conducted by Heritage and HBC or any shareholder, director, officer, employee or agent of Heritage or HBC;
perturbation.changed_text: Shareholder will not, directly or indirectly, ever make any statement that could be perceived negatively about Heritage and HBC, the business formerly conducted by Diablo, the business conducted by Heritage and HBC or any shareholder, director, officer, employee or agent of Heritage or HBC;
perturbation.explanation: The modified non-disparagement clause broadens the restriction to 'any statement that could be perceived negatively,' regardless of truth or context. This impinges on the Shareholder's right to free speech and expression regarding the company or its personnel.
perturbation.contradicted_law: First Amendment to the United States Constitution
perturbation.law_explanation: While non-disparagement clauses are common, this version goes too far by encompassing any potentially negative statement, regardless of veracity. This runs afoul of the First Amendment's guarantee of freedom of speech, as overly broad restrictions on speech are often deemed unconstitutional, especially if they inhibit the expression of truthful information or opinions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: First Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you First Amendment The First Amendment guarantees freedoms concerning religion, expression, assembly, and the right to petition. It forbids Congress from both promoting one religion over others and also restricting an individual’s religious practices . It guarantees freedom of expression by prohibiting Congress from restricting the press or the rights of individuals to speak freely. It also guarantees the right of citizens to assemble peaceably and to petition their government . Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment I Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion, or prohibiting the free exercise thereof; or abridging the freedom of speech, or of the press; or the right of the people peaceably to assemble, and to petition the government for a redress of grievances. perturbation.justification: The modified text expands the non-disparagement clause to include 'any statement that could be perceived negatively,' which the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation state infringes on the Shareholder's right to free speech. Scraped Law Snippet 2 affirms that the First Amendment guarantees freedom of expression. The original text restricts disparagement, whereas the changed text broadens this to any negatively perceived statement, thus restricting a wider range of speech and potentially violating the First Amendment's guarantee of free speech.
